cards.back: A C-style string is a char array terminated by a null character ('\0'). It's the traditional way to represent strings in C and older C++ code.

cards.text: C-style strings must end with a {{c1::null terminator}} which is the character {{c2::'\0'}} (value {{c3::0}})
To create a C-style string from a string literal: {{c1::const char* str = "Hello";}} or {{c2::char str[] = "Hello";}}

cards.question: How many elements does this array need?
cards.explanation: The compiler automatically adds the null terminator, so the array has 6 elements even though "Hello" has 5 visible characters.

cards.text: To use C-style string functions like strlen, strcpy, strcmp, include the {{c1::<cstring>}} header

cards.back: strlen() returns the number of characters in the C-style string, NOT including the null terminator. For "Hello", it returns 5, not 6.

cards.question: What's dangerous about this code?
cards.answer: Buffer overflow! "Hello" needs 6 chars (including '\0'), but array only has 5.
cards.explanation: strcpy doesn't check bounds. This writes past the array end, causing undefined behavior. Use strncpy or std::string instead.

cards.text: To compare two C-style strings, use {{c1::strcmp()}} NOT the {{c2::==}} operator, which compares pointer addresses

cards.back: Returns 0 if strings are equal, negative if str1 < str2, positive if str1 > str2 (lexicographically).

cards.text: To read a C-style string with std::cin, use {{c1::std::cin.getline(buffer, size)}} to prevent buffer overflow, NOT operator>>

cards.concept: Why avoid C-style strings in modern C++
cards.explanation:
C-style strings are error-prone:
  • No bounds checking (buffer overflows)
  • Manual memory management
  • Easy to forget null terminator
  • Pointer confusion with ==, =
  • No built-in length tracking
Use std::string or std::string_view instead.

cards.front: Why should string literals be stored as const char* instead of char*?
cards.back: String literals are stored in read-only memory. Modifying them is undefined behavior. const char* prevents accidental modification attempts.

cards.question: What's the issue here?
cards.answer: Prints "Hello" followed by garbage characters after the null terminator
cards.explanation: Should loop while str[i] != '\0' or use strlen(str) as the limit. The array has 10 elements but the string is only 6 chars including '\0'.
